title: ECG: left bundle branch block
body_html:
The diagram below shows the typical features of left bundle branch block (LBBB):


One of the most common ways to remember the difference between LBBB and RBBB is WiLLiaM MaRRoW
  • in LBBB there is a 'W' in V1 and a 'M' in V6
  • in RBBB there is a 'M' in V1 and a 'W' in V6

ECG showing typical features of LBBB. click on image to enlarge

Causes of LBBB
  • ischaemic heart disease
  • hypertension
  • aortic stenosis
  • cardiomyopathy
  • rare: idiopathic fibrosis, digoxin toxicity, hyperkalaemia

New LBBB is always pathological and may be a sign of a myocardial infarction. Diagnosing a myocardial infarction for patients with pre-existing LBBB is difficult. The Sgarbossa criteria can help with this. See below:

Sgarbossa criteria
CriteriaPoints if criteria met
Concordant ST elevation > 1mm in leads with a positive QRS complex5
Concordant ST depression > 1 mm in V1-V33
Excessively discordant ST elevation ≥5 mm in leads with a negative QRS)
2

Interpretation: A score of <3 is not considered diagnostic of acute MI but also does not rule out MI. Scores ≥ 3 have a specificity of 90% for diagnosing acute MI.

The Sgarbossa criteria were modified by Dr Smith (of Dr Smiths ECG blog fame) to become the modified Sgarbossa's criteria. In these criteria the third criteria in the table above has the original absolute 5 mm threshold replaced with a proportion: ST elevation/S-wave amplitude of ≤ -0.25.

In the modified criteria if any of the criterion are positive this supports a diagnosis of acute MI.

Modified Sgarbossa criteria
Criteria
Concordant ST elevation > 1mm in leads with a positive QRS complex
Concordant ST depression > 1 mm in V1-V3
ST Elevation at the J-point, relative to QRS onset, is at least 1 mm AND has an amplitude at least 25% of the preceding S-wave
American college of cardiology00
LBBB in Patients With Suspected MI: An Evolving Paradigm
2
2
Physiology
Cardiovascular physiology

Concordant ST elevation > 1mm in leads with a positive QRS complex5

Excessively discordant ST elevation ≥5 mm in leads with a negative QRS)
2

The Sgarbossa criteria were modified by Dr Smith (of Dr Smiths ECG blog fame) to become the modified Sgarbossa's criteria. In these criteria the third criteria in the table above has the original absolute 5 mm threshold replaced with a proportion: ST elevation/S-wave amplitude of ≤ -0.25.

Concordant ST elevation > 1mm in leads with a positive QRS complex

ST Elevation at the J-point, relative to QRS onset, is at least 1 mm AND has an amplitude at least 25% of the preceding S-wave
